name: fancy small war hammer
shortDescription: A compact war hammer dressed with polished collars, carved grip rings, and enough ornament to suggest ceremonial duty before battlefield use. It still has real weight behind the head, which makes it a favorite of buyers who want a courtly weapon that can survive rough roads.
flavorText: “Small enough for a noble's belt, heavy enough to settle a tavern argument.”

name: heavy war hammer
shortDescription: A brutal war hammer with a thick haft and a dense striking head meant for slow, committed blows. Smiths admire the balance, guards respect the damage it implies, and haulers complain about moving it from one stall to another.
flavorText: “The first swing is a warning. The second is usually bookkeeping.”

name: massive war hammer
shortDescription: An enormous war hammer built for giants, champions, or anyone trying very hard to look like one. Its head is broad, its shaft is reinforced, and its market value rises sharply whenever arena fighters pass through town.
flavorText: “Some weapons ask for strength. This one asks for witnesses.”

name: long war hammer
shortDescription: A long-handled war hammer made to keep danger at arm's length while still delivering ugly force at the end. Caravan guards like its reach, though it is awkward in tight shops and nearly impossible to lean neatly in a rack.
flavorText: “Distance is polite until the hammer arrives.”

name: small war hammer
shortDescription: A plain small war hammer, practical enough for close work and sturdy enough to be trusted after years of dents. It sells well to travelers who want something compact, serious, and less flashy than a sword.
flavorText: “No plume, no song, no legend. Just iron doing its job.”

name: dice
shortDescription: A handful of worn dice with rounded corners, scratched pips, and a history of being accused of dishonesty. Whether fair or not, they draw gamblers, bored caravan hands, and innkeepers who know games sell drinks.
flavorText: “The cheapest way to learn a man's optimism.”

name: checkered board
shortDescription: A folding checkered board marked by candle burns, travel stains, and pieces that have clearly changed owners many times. It is a quiet luxury for inns, camps, and shop counters where strategy is safer than shouting.
flavorText: “Wars are easier to enjoy when the armies fit in a pouch.”

name: marbles
shortDescription: A bright pouch of glass marbles, each one catching light like a tiny captured market day. Children want them, collectors sort them by swirl and color, and clever traders use them to keep negotiations with parents from ending too quickly.
flavorText: “Small fortunes roll farther than large ones.”

name: doll
shortDescription: A handmade doll with stitched clothes, painted features, and the soft wear of being carried from place to place. It sells as a toy, keepsake, or apology gift, depending on how guilty the buyer looks.
flavorText: “Some treasures are hugged until the seams complain.”

name: Crowncards Deck
shortDescription: A complete Crowncards deck wrapped in a stiff paper sleeve, used for games, fortunes, and arguments about rules no two ports agree on. The suits are crisp enough for play but weathered enough to feel lucky.
flavorText: “Every table has a champion until the deck is shuffled.”

name: Crowncards Tide Card
shortDescription: The Tide card from a Crowncards set, showing waves curling around a silver crown. Sailors buy it as a charm, gamblers call it a sign of reversal, and mystics insist it means whatever the tide is already doing.
flavorText: “It promises change, then refuses to say when.”

name: Crowncards Sun Card
shortDescription: The Sun card from a Crowncards deck, bright with gold ink and cheerful arrogance. Players prize it in certain games, fortune-tellers display it near their best cloth, and merchants like how quickly it catches the eye.
flavorText: “A small sunrise, cheaply laminated by faith.”

name: Crowncards Star Card
shortDescription: The Star card bears a pale crown under a scatter of night lights, a favorite among travelers and hopeful apprentices. Its corners are often rubbed for luck before wagers, journeys, or foolish promises.
flavorText: “A card for those who mistake distance for destiny.”

name: Crowncards Moon Card
shortDescription: The Moon card is inked in silver-gray tones, its crowned crescent half hidden behind clouds. It is popular with night dealers, fortune stalls, and anyone who wants mystery at a fair price.
flavorText: “Best played after sunset, when everyone lies more softly.”

name: Crowncards Ember Card
shortDescription: The Ember card shows a tiny red crown glowing among ash, prized in games that reward patience before a sudden turn. Its color makes it easy to spot, which has caused more than one accusation across tavern tables.
flavorText: “A quiet card until it burns the hand that ignored it.”

name: Crowncards Clock Card
shortDescription: The Clock card is marked with a crowned dial and neat little gears, a favorite of rule-minded players who enjoy timing, turns, and traps. Merchants use it as a desk token when appointments are being missed.
flavorText: “The only card that makes late customers feel judged.”

name: Crowncards Cloud Card
shortDescription: The Cloud card is painted in soft grays and drifting whites, vague enough to make fortune-tellers sound profound. In play, it is loved by cautious hands and disliked by anyone who wants a clean answer.
flavorText: “A card with no edges, just excuses.”

name: Crowncards Fish Card
shortDescription: The Fish card shows a crowned little swimmer flashing blue-green scales. Dockside players favor it, children like the picture, and superstitious cooks keep one near the till during poor fishing weeks.
flavorText: “It slips from bad hands and somehow feeds good ones.”

name: Crowncards Sprout Card
shortDescription: The Sprout card carries a tiny crown above new green leaves, making it popular with gardeners, lenders, and optimists. It is a modest card, but many games turn on modest things at the right moment.
flavorText: “Small beginnings are easier to sell than large apologies.”

name: Crowncards Frost Card
shortDescription: The Frost card is edged in pale blue ink, with a crown caught in sharp winter patterns. Players use it to slow a table's rhythm, while fortune readers present it when they need bad news to sound elegant.
flavorText: “Cold luck is still luck, if it freezes your enemy first.”

name: Crowncards Dream Card
shortDescription: The Dream card shows a soft crown floating above impossible towers, blurred just enough to seem important. It is favored by poets, sleepy gamblers, and buyers who believe a deck should have at least one beautiful lie.
flavorText: “Do not ask what it means before breakfast.”

name: Crowncards Heart Card
shortDescription: The Heart card is warm red and carefully worn along the edges, often kept as a token more than a playing piece. Lovers, healers, and theatrical gamblers all overpay for one in decent condition.
flavorText: “The most folded card in every deck.”

name: Crowncards Madness Card
shortDescription: The Madness card is a riot of crooked crowns, bright lines, and faces that may not have been printed in the same direction twice. It draws collectors, risk-heavy players, and anyone who thinks bad ideas deserve props.
flavorText: “A warning label disguised as entertainment.”

name: Crowncards Lightning Card
shortDescription: The Lightning card flashes with jagged yellow marks around a black crown, dramatic enough to make even dull hands look dangerous. Players slap it down with unnecessary force, which is why clean copies are rare.
flavorText: “Fast luck leaves scorch marks.”

name: Crowncards Harvest Card
shortDescription: The Harvest card shows a crown resting among sheaves of grain, steady and prosperous in the old merchant way. It sells well before fairs, tax days, and any season when farmers are feeling hopeful.
flavorText: “A good card to draw, a better card to own.”

name: Crowncards Hare Card
shortDescription: The Hare card features a quick little creature with a tilted crown and very alert ears. It is loved by children, trick players, and messengers who enjoy suggesting that speed is a virtue rather than a problem.
flavorText: “It wins by leaving before the argument begins.”

name: Crowncards Peace Card
shortDescription: The Peace card is quiet, pale, and often mistaken for weak until the rules prove otherwise. Priests, diplomats, and tired innkeepers buy copies for symbolism, though gamblers mostly keep it for strategy.
flavorText: “The table goes silent when peace becomes profitable.”

name: Crowncards War Card
shortDescription: The War card shows crossed blades beneath a heavy crown, with red ink darkened from years of handling. It is the loudest card in many decks, even before players start explaining why they deserved to win.
flavorText: “Every tavern general knows this one by touch.”

name: Crowncards Fox Card
shortDescription: The Fox card wears a sly little crown and a look of complete innocence. Traders love it, cheats fear it, and honest players pretend not to enjoy it when the table turns suddenly clever.
flavorText: “Never trust a card that looks pleased with itself.”

name: Crowncards Lantern Card
shortDescription: The Lantern card glows with warm ink around a small crowned flame, often saved for last in storytelling games. It is prized by night travelers and fortune readers who prefer hope with a handle.
flavorText: “A little light, useful mostly because the dark is so ambitious.”

name: brass trade token
shortDescription: A stamped brass trade token accepted by certain stalls, guildhouses, and stubborn old market clerks. Its value depends on place and promise rather than metal, so clever merchants check the mark before smiling.
flavorText: “Worth a loaf in one street and a lecture in the next.”

name: sealed credit note
shortDescription: A folded credit note closed with wax and careful handwriting, promising payment from someone with enough reputation to make paper feel heavy. It trades best among bankers, caravans, and people who still believe seals behave honestly.
flavorText: “Ink can be heavier than coin when the right name signs it.”

name: dockmaster tally strip
shortDescription: A narrow tally strip marked by a dockmaster to track crates, berths, fees, and favors owed. It looks unimpressive until a ship captain realizes it can prove who paid, who lied, and who is still waiting.
flavorText: “At the docks, memory is written in scratches and salt.”

name: caravan bond
shortDescription: A caravan bond binding goods, guards, and payment into one suspiciously tidy promise. Traders buy it for security, lenders buy it for leverage, and everyone reads the weather clause twice.
flavorText: “A road contract is a prayer with witnesses.”

name: pearl backed promissory note
shortDescription: A fine promissory note backed by a pearl deposit, written on thick paper that wants everyone to notice its importance. It travels carefully between coastal houses, jewel brokers, and creditors with soft voices.
flavorText: “The pearl is elsewhere, which is what makes the paper nervous.”

name: jewel weight scale set
shortDescription: A delicate set of jewel scales with tiny pans, calibrated weights, and a case lined to keep every piece from wandering. Gem cutters and honest dealers need it; dishonest dealers need one that looks like it.
flavorText: “Trust begins where the needle stops trembling.”

name: merchant seal kit
shortDescription: A merchant seal kit with wax sticks, a small press, and space for a personal mark or house stamp. It turns ordinary bundles into accountable goods and gives even cheap letters the posture of authority.
flavorText: “A seal says nothing changed after you looked away.”

name: oat loaf
shortDescription: A dense oat loaf with a rough crust and honest, filling weight. It does not flatter the table, but travelers, laborers, and practical innkeepers value bread that lasts longer than compliments.
flavorText: “Not fancy. Still gone by supper.”

name: barley flatbread
shortDescription: A round barley flatbread, lightly browned and sturdy enough for stew, cheese, or whatever a roadside meal has become. It stacks neatly, travels well, and rarely causes anyone to ask for a plate.
flavorText: “The plate you can eat is the superior plate.”

name: honeycrust roll
shortDescription: A small roll glazed with honey until the crust shines and sticks faintly to the fingers. It sells fastest near children, courting benches, and tired buyers who intended to purchase something sensible.
flavorText: “Sweetness is the cheapest shortcut to generosity.”

name: salt bun
shortDescription: A pale bun topped with coarse salt, simple but dangerously satisfying when eaten warm. Dockhands and guards buy them by the pair, claiming the salt keeps them alert, which sounds official enough.
flavorText: “A humble bun with the confidence of a ration.”

name: moonbun pastry
shortDescription: A crescent-shaped moonbun pastry filled with sweet paste and dusted just enough to mark a clean sleeve. Festival stalls sell them as treats, but night markets call them lucky and charge accordingly.
flavorText: “Best eaten under moonlight, or behind the stall before anyone notices.”

name: orchard pie
shortDescription: A small orchard pie packed with soft fruit, spice, and a crust that flakes when treated kindly. Its scent can interrupt negotiations, which makes it useful to bakers and dangerous to hungry appraisers.
flavorText: “A pie is just a contract between fruit and patience.”

name: sailor hardcake
shortDescription: A tough sailor hardcake baked for long storage, hard chewing, and storms that ignore dinner plans. It is not beloved for flavor, but it survives damp holds better than most promises.
flavorText: “Break a tooth today so hunger does not break you tomorrow.”

name: festival sugar twist
shortDescription: A curled strip of fried dough and sugar, bright enough for fairs and sticky enough to mark every hand that touches it. It is cheap joy in spiral form and a reliable way to draw foot traffic.
flavorText: “No one bargains fiercely while licking sugar from their thumb.”

name: amber wheat sack
shortDescription: A full sack of amber wheat, cleanly tied and warm in color, ready for millers, bakers, or careful storage. Buyers check for damp first, then insects, then whether the seller looks too eager.
flavorText: “Good wheat smells like tomorrow's bread.”

name: blue barley sack
shortDescription: A sack of blue barley with a cool tint that makes it stand out among ordinary grain goods. Brewers ask for it first, cooks ask questions second, and merchants enjoy anything that looks rare before it is weighed.
flavorText: “Strange color, steady grain, excellent excuse for a higher price.”

name: red millet sack
shortDescription: A red millet sack filled with small hardy grains prized for porridge, feed, and lean travel kitchens. It is humble stock, but in hungry districts humble stock becomes the backbone of trade.
flavorText: “Tiny grains, large arguments over the last scoop.”

name: moonpea seed packet
shortDescription: A packet of moonpea seeds wrapped in waxed paper and marked with careful planting notes. Gardeners like their pale blossoms, cooks like the harvest, and hedge witches like the name a little too much.
flavorText: “Plant them after dusk if you enjoy unnecessary traditions.”

name: saffron crocus bulbs
shortDescription: A bundle of saffron crocus bulbs, dry, papery, and far more valuable than their plain look suggests. Patient growers prize them for the spice to come, while impatient merchants simply count the bulbs twice.
flavorText: “The richest color in the room begins as a quiet little bulb.”
